title: Hypocalcaemia — symptomatic
oneLiner: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia is a medical emergency causing tetany, seizures, or cardiac arrhythmia due to low ionised calcium – correct fast, but don’t miss the cause.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Laryngospasm / respiratory arrest** – stridor, hypoxia, inability to speak – treat immediately with IV calcium even before confirmatory labs.
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (torsades de pointes)** – prolonged QT on ECG, syncope or cardiac arrest – give IV calcium and magnesium, call cardiac arrest team.
- **Seizure (generalised or focal)** – distinguish from other causes by presence of perioral tingling, Chvostek’s sign, or carpopedal spasm.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (the 5 you’ll actually see):**
  - Perioral numbness / tingling (earliest symptom)
  - Carpopedal spasm (“main d’accoucheur”)
  - Muscular cramps / myalgia
  - Tetany (generalised spontaneous muscle contractions)
  - Seizures (can be first presentation in severe cases)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Chvostek’s sign: tapping over facial nerve (anterior to ear) – twitching of ipsilateral facial muscles.
  - Trousseau’s sign: inflate BP cuff > systolic for 3 min – carpal spasm.
  - Hyperreflexia, clonus
  - Prolonged QT on ECG (QTc > 470 ms)
  - Papilloedema (rare, chronic)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Hypoparathyroidism (post-thyroidectomy / autoimmune) – low PTH, normal Mg, history of neck surgery.
  - Vitamin D deficiency / osteomalacia – raised ALP, low 25-OH vitamin D, often low phosphate.
  - Chronic kidney disease – high phosphate, raised urea/creatinine, low GFR.
  - Acute pancreatitis – epigastric pain, raised amylase/lipase.
  - Magnesium deficiency – causes refractory hypocalcaemia (check Mg first).
  - Sepsis / critical illness – ionised calcium low, total calcium may be normal.
  - Rhabdomyolysis / tumour lysis – history of crush injury, recent chemo, hyperuricaemia.
- **Splitter:** Check PTH – if low or inappropriately normal → hypoparathyroidism; if high → secondary hyperparathyroidism (vitamin D deficiency, CKD, malabsorption).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (look for prolonged QT, arrhythmia)
  - Blood gas (ionised calcium – more reliable than total)
  - Assess Chvostek’s / Trousseau’s
  - Check IV access, monitor vitals
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine, Mg, PO4, albumin (corrected calcium formula if using total Ca)
  - PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, 1,25 dihydroxyvitamin D
  - Amylase (if pancreatitis suspected)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - If post-thyroidectomy: USS neck for haematoma / parathyroid damage
  - If suspected bone disease: plain X-ray hands/wrists (osteomalacia)
  - CT head if seizure with no clear cause
- **Special tests:**
  - Ca²⁺/creatinine clearance ratio (to distinguish familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia – rare)
  - Genetic testing if young, recurrent, family history
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Symptomatic hypocalcaemia: corrected total Ca < 2.1 mmol/L OR ionised Ca < 1.1 mmol/L
  - Severe: < 1.9 mmol/L (or ionised < 0.9) – treat urgently
- **Never-miss test:** **Magnesium level** – hypomagnesaemia is the most common correctable cause of refractory hypocalcaemia.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Ensure ABCDE, apply O₂ if hypoxic
  - Corrected calcium < 1.9 mmol/L OR symptomatic → **IV 10% calcium gluconate**
  - [Check local policy for dose] Typically 10–20 mL IV slowly over 10–20 min while monitoring ECG
  - If severe / periarrest: **IV calcium chloride** (more potent, but irritant – central line only)
  - Give **IV magnesium sulphate** if Mg < 0.7 mmol/L – [check local policy] (e.g. 2 g IV over 10 min)
  - Treat seizures with benzodiazepines (diazepam or lorazepam) first, then correct Ca
  - For laryngospasm: call anaesthetist; give IV calcium + bag-mask if needed
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Recheck corrected calcium 1–2 hours after IV bolus – give repeat doses if still low
  - Start **IV calcium infusion** if ongoing requirement: [check local policy] – e.g. 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate in 100 mL 0.9% NaCl, run slowly (100 mL/h) with cardiac monitoring
  - Check Mg, PO4, K+ daily
  - Identify and treat underlying cause:
    - Hypoparathyroidism: long-term oral calcium + activated vitamin D (calcitriol / alfacalcidol)
    - Vitamin D deficiency: high-dose cholecalciferol (e.g. 300,000 IU stat)
    - CKD: consider phosphate binder, vitamin D analogue
    - Pancreatitis: supportive care, treat cause
  - For post-thyroidectomy: give oral calcium ± vitamin D from day 1
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If chronic hypoparathyroidism: oral calcium + calcitriol, monitor Ca, PO4, and 24h urine Ca
  - If vitamin D deficiency: maintenance cholecalciferol (e.g. 800–2000 IU daily) and repeat levels in 3 months
  - For CKD: refer to renal team for phosphate management and vitamin D analogue
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (unless fluid overload)
  - VTE prophylaxis: as per local protocol (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily)
  - Diet: normal unless pancreatitis/NBM
  - Observations: 4-hourly cardiac monitoring until corrected Ca stable > 2.0 mmol/L
  - Falls risk assessment
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Calcium gluconate 10%** – 10–20 mL IV slowly over 10–20 min (in the ED/ward). Repeat as needed.
    - [Check local policy for maximum infusion rate to avoid bradycardia]
  - **Calcium chloride 10%** – 5–10 mL IV via central line only (peripheral extravasation causes necrosis)
  - **IV magnesium sulphate** – 2 g in 10 mL 0.9% NaCl IV over 10–20 min (if Mg < 0.7)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oral calcium carbonate 1.5–3 g elemental calcium/day in divided doses (if able to swallow)
  - Calcitriol 0.25–1.0 mcg PO daily (for hypoparathyroidism / CKD)
  - Alfacalcidol 0.25–1.0 mcg PO daily (alternative)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Calcium + digoxin → risk of digoxin toxicity (monitor levels, consider slower infusion)
  - Calcium + thiazide diuretics → hypercalcaemia
  - IV calcium + ceftriaxone → precipitation (do not give in same line)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment for calcium (IV/PO) in renal impairment – use cautiously if hyperphosphataemia
  - Calcitriol/alfacalcidol: reduce dose if hypercalcaemia develops; adjust in CKD as per local protocol
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV calcium too fast** – can cause bradycardia, cardiac arrest. Always infuse over at least 10 min with ECG monitoring. Avoid concurrent digoxin.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (VT, torsades, bradycardia, heart block)
  - Laryngospasm → respiratory arrest
  - Seizures → aspiration, hypoxia
  - IV calcium extravasation → skin necrosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Ectopic calcification (brain, kidneys, vessels) – especially in hypoparathyroidism
  - Basal ganglia calcification → movement disorders
  - Cataracts
  - Impaired renal function (nephrocalcinosis)
sections.Prognosis: - If treated promptly, symptomatic hypocalcaemia resolves quickly. Mortality is low (<5%) unless underlying sepsis/pancreatitis/malignancy is severe. Chronic hypoparathyroidism requires lifelong treatment with risk of renal complications. Vitamin D deficiency has excellent prognosis with replacement.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check magnesium before finalising treatment plan** – if Mg is low, hypocalcaemia will not correct until Mg is replaced.
- **Use ionised calcium on blood gas** for real-time decision-making in symptomatic patients – it is not affected by albumin.
- **If post-thyroidectomy patient develops tetany**, give IV calcium immediately, then call the surgical team – could be parathyroid injury needing long-term replacement.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not use total calcium alone without albumin correction** – in hypoalbuminaemia, total calcium may be falsely low. Use corrected formula or ionised.
- **Do not give IV calcium without ECG monitoring** – risk of arrhythmia; if patient is on digoxin, even more careful.
- **Do not stop looking for the cause after treating symptoms** – hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, renal disease each have different long-term management.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any recurrence of perioral tingling, muscle cramps, difficulty breathing, palpitations, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** GP for repeat calcium, magnesium, vitamin D levels in 1–2 weeks. If hypoparathyroidism, refer to endocrinology. If vitamin D deficiency, check levels after 3 months.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/SpR):** if corrected Ca < 1.9 mmol/L despite initial bolus; if seizures/laryngospasm; if on digoxin; if unknown cause and no PTH results available.
- **Call ICU/crash team:** if cardiac arrest, ventricular arrhythmia, airway compromise, or refractory hypotension.
- **NEWS2 threshold:** score ≥ 5 or any single parameter ≥ 3 (especially respiratory rate, consciousness, SpO₂). But remember – hypocalcaemia may not cause high NEWS; use clinical judgement.

rawMarkdown:
## Hypocalcaemia — Symptomatic

**One-liner:** Symptomatic hypocalcaemia is a medical emergency causing tetany, seizures, or cardiac arrhythmia due to low ionised calcium – correct fast, but don’t miss the cause.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Laryngospasm / respiratory arrest** – stridor, hypoxia, inability to speak – treat immediately with IV calcium even before confirmatory labs.
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (torsades de pointes)** – prolonged QT on ECG, syncope or cardiac arrest – give IV calcium and magnesium, call cardiac arrest team.
- **Seizure (generalised or focal)** – distinguish from other causes by presence of perioral tingling, Chvostek’s sign, or carpopedal spasm.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (the 5 you’ll actually see):**
  - Perioral numbness / tingling (earliest symptom)
  - Carpopedal spasm (“main d’accoucheur”)
  - Muscular cramps / myalgia
  - Tetany (generalised spontaneous muscle contractions)
  - Seizures (can be first presentation in severe cases)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Chvostek’s sign: tapping over facial nerve (anterior to ear) – twitching of ipsilateral facial muscles.
  - Trousseau’s sign: inflate BP cuff > systolic for 3 min – carpal spasm.
  - Hyperreflexia, clonus
  - Prolonged QT on ECG (QTc > 470 ms)
  - Papilloedema (rare, chronic)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Hypoparathyroidism (post-thyroidectomy / autoimmune) – low PTH, normal Mg, history of neck surgery.
  - Vitamin D deficiency / osteomalacia – raised ALP, low 25-OH vitamin D, often low phosphate.
  - Chronic kidney disease – high phosphate, raised urea/creatinine, low GFR.
  - Acute pancreatitis – epigastric pain, raised amylase/lipase.
  - Magnesium deficiency – causes refractory hypocalcaemia (check Mg first).
  - Sepsis / critical illness – ionised calcium low, total calcium may be normal.
  - Rhabdomyolysis / tumour lysis – history of crush injury, recent chemo, hyperuricaemia.
- **Splitter:** Check PTH – if low or inappropriately normal → hypoparathyroidism; if high → secondary hyperparathyroidism (vitamin D deficiency, CKD, malabsorption).

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (look for prolonged QT, arrhythmia)
  - Blood gas (ionised calcium – more reliable than total)
  - Assess Chvostek’s / Trousseau’s
  - Check IV access, monitor vitals
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine, Mg, PO4, albumin (corrected calcium formula if using total Ca)
  - PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, 1,25 dihydroxyvitamin D
  - Amylase (if pancreatitis suspected)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - If post-thyroidectomy: USS neck for haematoma / parathyroid damage
  - If suspected bone disease: plain X-ray hands/wrists (osteomalacia)
  - CT head if seizure with no clear cause
- **Special tests:**
  - Ca²⁺/creatinine clearance ratio (to distinguish familial hypocalciuric hypercalcaemia – rare)
  - Genetic testing if young, recurrent, family history
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Symptomatic hypocalcaemia: corrected total Ca < 2.1 mmol/L OR ionised Ca < 1.1 mmol/L
  - Severe: < 1.9 mmol/L (or ionised < 0.9) – treat urgently
- **Never-miss test:** **Magnesium level** – hypomagnesaemia is the most common correctable cause of refractory hypocalcaemia.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Ensure ABCDE, apply O₂ if hypoxic
  - Corrected calcium < 1.9 mmol/L OR symptomatic → **IV 10% calcium gluconate**
  - [Check local policy for dose] Typically 10–20 mL IV slowly over 10–20 min while monitoring ECG
  - If severe / periarrest: **IV calcium chloride** (more potent, but irritant – central line only)
  - Give **IV magnesium sulphate** if Mg < 0.7 mmol/L – [check local policy] (e.g. 2 g IV over 10 min)
  - Treat seizures with benzodiazepines (diazepam or lorazepam) first, then correct Ca
  - For laryngospasm: call anaesthetist; give IV calcium + bag-mask if needed
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Recheck corrected calcium 1–2 hours after IV bolus – give repeat doses if still low
  - Start **IV calcium infusion** if ongoing requirement: [check local policy] – e.g. 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate in 100 mL 0.9% NaCl, run slowly (100 mL/h) with cardiac monitoring
  - Check Mg, PO4, K+ daily
  - Identify and treat underlying cause:
    - Hypoparathyroidism: long-term oral calcium + activated vitamin D (calcitriol / alfacalcidol)
    - Vitamin D deficiency: high-dose cholecalciferol (e.g. 300,000 IU stat)
    - CKD: consider phosphate binder, vitamin D analogue
    - Pancreatitis: supportive care, treat cause
  - For post-thyroidectomy: give oral calcium ± vitamin D from day 1
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - If chronic hypoparathyroidism: oral calcium + calcitriol, monitor Ca, PO4, and 24h urine Ca
  - If vitamin D deficiency: maintenance cholecalciferol (e.g. 800–2000 IU daily) and repeat levels in 3 months
  - For CKD: refer to renal team for phosphate management and vitamin D analogue
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at 125 mL/h (unless fluid overload)
  - VTE prophylaxis: as per local protocol (enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily)
  - Diet: normal unless pancreatitis/NBM
  - Observations: 4-hourly cardiac monitoring until corrected Ca stable > 2.0 mmol/L
  - Falls risk assessment

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Calcium gluconate 10%** – 10–20 mL IV slowly over 10–20 min (in the ED/ward). Repeat as needed.
    - [Check local policy for maximum infusion rate to avoid bradycardia]
  - **Calcium chloride 10%** – 5–10 mL IV via central line only (peripheral extravasation causes necrosis)
  - **IV magnesium sulphate** – 2 g in 10 mL 0.9% NaCl IV over 10–20 min (if Mg < 0.7)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oral calcium carbonate 1.5–3 g elemental calcium/day in divided doses (if able to swallow)
  - Calcitriol 0.25–1.0 mcg PO daily (for hypoparathyroidism / CKD)
  - Alfacalcidol 0.25–1.0 mcg PO daily (alternative)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Calcium + digoxin → risk of digoxin toxicity (monitor levels, consider slower infusion)
  - Calcium + thiazide diuretics → hypercalcaemia
  - IV calcium + ceftriaxone → precipitation (do not give in same line)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment for calcium (IV/PO) in renal impairment – use cautiously if hyperphosphataemia
  - Calcitriol/alfacalcidol: reduce dose if hypercalcaemia develops; adjust in CKD as per local protocol
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV calcium too fast** – can cause bradycardia, cardiac arrest. Always infuse over at least 10 min with ECG monitoring. Avoid concurrent digoxin.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cardiac arrhythmia (VT, torsades, bradycardia, heart block)
  - Laryngospasm → respiratory arrest
  - Seizures → aspiration, hypoxia
  - IV calcium extravasation → skin necrosis
- **Chronic:**
  - Ectopic calcification (brain, kidneys, vessels) – especially in hypoparathyroidism
  - Basal ganglia calcification → movement disorders
  - Cataracts
  - Impaired renal function (nephrocalcinosis)

### Prognosis
- If treated promptly, symptomatic hypocalcaemia resolves quickly. Mortality is low (<5%) unless underlying sepsis/pancreatitis/malignancy is severe. Chronic hypoparathyroidism requires lifelong treatment with risk of renal complications. Vitamin D deficiency has excellent prognosis with replacement.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check magnesium before finalising treatment plan** – if Mg is low, hypocalcaemia will not correct until Mg is replaced.
- **Use ionised calcium on blood gas** for real-time decision-making in symptomatic patients – it is not affected by albumin.
- **If post-thyroidectomy patient develops tetany**, give IV calcium immediately, then call the surgical team – could be parathyroid injury needing long-term replacement.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not use total calcium alone without albumin correction** – in hypoalbuminaemia, total calcium may be falsely low. Use corrected formula or ionised.
- **Do not give IV calcium without ECG monitoring** – risk of arrhythmia; if patient is on digoxin, even more careful.
- **Do not stop looking for the cause after treating symptoms** – hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, renal disease each have different long-term management.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any recurrence of perioral tingling, muscle cramps, difficulty breathing, palpitations, or seizure.
- **Follow-up:** GP for repeat calcium, magnesium, vitamin D levels in 1–2 weeks. If hypoparathyroidism, refer to endocrinology. If vitamin D deficiency, check levels after 3 months.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/SpR):** if corrected Ca < 1.9 mmol/L despite initial bolus; if seizures/laryngospasm; if on digoxin; if unknown cause and no PTH results available.
- **Call ICU/crash team:** if cardiac arrest, ventricular arrhythmia, airway compromise, or refractory hypotension.
- **NEWS2 threshold:** score ≥ 5 or any single parameter ≥ 3 (especially respiratory rate, consciousness, SpO₂). But remember – hypocalcaemia may not cause high NEWS; use clinical judgement.
